stem: A soil test predicts the presence of contaminants with 88% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 89% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 98% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 98% chance they're actually contaminated.' The base rate of samples from actually contaminated sites in this soil samples pool is estimated between 1.00% and 2.40%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged soil sample is truly contaminated? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(contaminated | flagged) ranges from 0.0826 to 0.18 (NOT 98%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 1.70%: 0.134.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | contaminated) = 0.98 with P(contaminated | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.134) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.98) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 98% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
